crossref.abstract:
<jats:p><jats:italic>Context.</jats:italic> 3C 84 is a nearby radio source with a complex total intensity structure, showing linear polarisation and spectral patterns. A detailed investigation of the central engine region necessitates the use of very-long-baseline interferometry (VLBI) above the hitherto available maximum frequency of 86 GHz.</jats:p>
<jats:p><jats:italic>Aims.</jats:italic> Using ultrahigh resolution VLBI observations at the currently highest available frequency of 228 GHz, we aim to perform a direct detection of compact structures and understand the physical conditions in the compact region of 3C 84.</jats:p>
<jats:p><jats:italic>Methods.</jats:italic> We used Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) 228 GHz observations and, given the limited (<jats:italic>u</jats:italic>, <jats:italic>v</jats:italic>)-coverage, applied geometric model fitting to the data. Furthermore, we employed quasi-simultaneously observed, ancillary multi-frequency VLBI data for the source in order to carry out a comprehensive analysis of the core structure.</jats:p>
<jats:p><jats:italic>Results.</jats:italic> We report the detection of a highly ordered, strong magnetic field around the central, supermassive black hole of 3C 84. The brightness temperature analysis suggests that the system is in equipartition. We also determined a turnover frequency of <jats:italic>ν</jats:italic><jats:sub>m</jats:sub> = (113 ± 4) GHz, a corresponding synchrotron self-absorbed magnetic field of <jats:italic>B</jats:italic><jats:sub>SSA</jats:sub> = (2.9 ± 1.6) G, and an equipartition magnetic field of <jats:italic>B</jats:italic><jats:sub>eq</jats:sub> = (5.2 ± 0.6) G. Three components are resolved with the highest fractional polarisation detected for this object (<jats:italic>m</jats:italic><jats:sub>net</jats:sub> = (17.0 ± 3.9)%). The positions of the components are compatible with those seen in low-frequency VLBI observations since 2017–2018. We report a steeply negative slope of the spectrum at 228 GHz. We used these findings to test existing models of jet formation, propagation, and Faraday rotation in 3C 84.</jats:p>
<jats:p><jats:italic>Conclusions.</jats:italic> The findings of our investigation into different flow geometries and black hole spins support an advection-dominated accretion flow in a magnetically arrested state around a rapidly rotating supermassive black hole as a model of the jet-launching system in the core of 3C 84. However, systematic uncertainties due to the limited (<jats:italic>u</jats:italic>, <jats:italic>v</jats:italic>)-coverage, however, cannot be ignored. Our upcoming work using new EHT data, which offer full imaging capabilities, will shed more light on the compact region of 3C 84.</jats:p>
title: Ordered magnetic fields around the 3C 84 central black hole
